article.title: NASA Begins Countdown for Artemis II, the First Crewed Lunar Mission in 54 Years, With April 1 Launch Targeted
article.summary: Four astronauts will fly around the Moon aboard the SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft, marking humanity's first crewed voyage beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's countdown clock for the Artemis II mission began ticking at 4:44 p.m. EDT on March 30, setting the stage for a targeted launch at 6:24 p.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 1, from Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center in Florida, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/). If the launch proceeds as planned, four astronauts will become the first humans to travel beyond low Earth orbit since the Apollo 17 crew returned from the lunar surface in December 1972 -- a gap of more than 54 years.

The 10-day mission will send the crew approximately 685,000 miles around the Moon and back aboard NASA's Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion spacecraft, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-tuesday-march-31). Artemis II is a test flight designed to validate the crewed systems before NASA attempts lunar surface landings in subsequent missions.

## The Crew

The four-person crew represents several historic firsts, as reported by [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-tuesday-march-31):

- **Commander Reid Wiseman** (NASA) -- A former U.S. Navy fighter pilot from Baltimore who previously spent 165 days aboard the International Space Station in 2014.
- **Pilot Victor Glover** (NASA) -- A former U.S. Navy captain and F/A-18 Hornet combat pilot from California, who will become the first Black astronaut assigned to a lunar mission.
- **Mission Specialist Christina Koch** (NASA) -- From Grand Rapids, Michigan, Koch holds the NASA record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days and participated in the first all-female spacewalk.
- **Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency) -- A former Royal Canadian Air Force fighter pilot embarking on his first spaceflight, making him the first non-American to fly to the lunar vicinity.

All four crew members are currently in pre-flight quarantine at Kennedy Space Center, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/29/final-preparations-underway-for-nasas-moon-mission/).

## Mission Profile

The SLS rocket stands 322 feet tall and produces 8.8 million pounds of thrust at liftoff -- roughly 15 percent more than the Saturn V rockets that carried Apollo astronauts to the Moon, according to [Space.com](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-31-2026). The launch sequence involves loading hundreds of thousands of gallons of super-cooled liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen into the rocket's core stage, with a sound suppression water deluge system protecting the pad at ignition, as described by [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/).

The mission profile calls for a lunar flyby rather than a landing. The Orion spacecraft will venture approximately 5,000 miles beyond the lunar surface before swinging back toward Earth, according to [Space.com](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-31-2026). Upon reentry, the crew will be traveling at over 25,000 mph, which [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-tuesday-march-31) notes would surpass the speed record set by Apollo 10's crew in 1969, making them the fastest humans in history.

## Launch Weather and Backup Windows

NASA's weather team has assessed an 80 percent chance of favorable conditions for the April 1 launch window, with cloud coverage and potential high winds on the ground identified as the primary concerns, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/weather-for-nasas-artemis-ii-mission-launch-80-favorable/). The two-hour launch window opens at 6:24 p.m. EDT, and if weather or technical issues force a delay, backup opportunities exist through April 6.

## What We Don't Know

While the mission hardware has been tested extensively -- including the uncrewed Artemis I flight in 2022 that sent an empty Orion capsule around the Moon -- Artemis II will be the first time astronauts ride the SLS. Commander Wiseman has acknowledged the inherent uncertainty, stating that "this is a test mission" and that the crew may need to abort and return home early depending on how systems perform, as reported by [Space.com](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-31-2026).

It also remains to be seen whether the broader Artemis timeline will hold. Lunar surface landings under Artemis III and IV are planned for 2028, and NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman has announced a 20 billion dollar, seven-year budget for lunar base construction near the south pole, according to [Space.com](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-31-2026). Whether Congress sustains that funding across multiple budget cycles is an open question.

## Why It Matters

Artemis II is more than a test flight. It represents the first tangible step in returning humans to the Moon since the Apollo era, with the broader goal of establishing a sustained presence on the lunar surface as a stepping stone toward eventual crewed missions to Mars. The crew's diversity -- including the first woman, first Black astronaut, and first non-American on a lunar trajectory -- also marks a symbolic departure from the Apollo program's exclusively white, male, American crews.
